The decisive factor for the dogfood-v2 "be bold, build foundation" mandate: this variant unblocks the named downstream priorities (morning brief, timetable, smart capture) because they can all import urgency()/useNow() rather than reinventing thresholds. Variant 1 is the surgical, lower-risk choice but stops at one rebuilt widget and leaves urgency logic un-reusable; for a foundation round that is exactly the wrong altitude. The trade-off (more new code up front, opinionated initial time UX) is acceptable because the data risk is identical to variant 1 and the custom fallback covers my edge cases.

experiments.priority: Win minute one AND make Today auto-generate (cold start + Morning Brief, shipped together)

variants.name: Chip Intake + Brief Banner (the single-screen seam)
variants.approach:
TIGHTEST, lowest-risk read of the brief. ONE new flow screen and ONE new home banner, both bolted onto seams that already exist.

(1) Cold start: extend WelcomeThemePicker into a 2-step welcome. After the dark/light card pick (which already calls chooseWelcomeMood in Dashboard.tsx:340), advance in-place to a chip-intake panel instead of dismissing. Show a grid of ~10 tappable life-area chips (Uni/Study, Work, Job Hunt, Gym, Money, Social, Side project, Home, Travel, Health) each carrying a baked-in {name, color, icon, description, keywords}. Tapping toggles selection; a one-line 'add your own' text input appends a custom chip. 'Done' (or a 60s implicit skip) calls a new Dashboard handler seedFromChips(selected): for each chip, build a real Topic via worldToTopic against the matching BUNDLED_WORLD (Study->'study', Job Hunt->'job-hunt') or a synthesized BozzWorld for chips without a bundled world, then setTopics([...]) and stamp 1-2 sample timed deadlines per topic via addTopicItem(topicId, sampleText, deadlineMs). Crucially write the chip's description+keywords onto each Topic so predictTopic scores >1 from day one (topicScore weights description terms +3, keywords +6). Then apply the existing 'essentials' STARTER_TEMPLATE so the home is populated, and set onboardingDismissed=true so the 4-walkthrough card never gates a now-populated home.

(2) Morning Brief: add a MorningBrief banner component rendered in HomeView directly under the greeting() line (HomeView.tsx:223/314), above the grid, driven PURELY by ctx.todayEvents (already aggregated in Dashboard.tsx:750 from topicDeadlineEvents+feed+notes). It renders: next timed event ('Next: 14:00 Lecture'), the single most-urgent deadline with a live ticking countdown (setInterval re-render), weather (reuse the weather fetch the WeatherWidget uses), and one synthesized focus line ('Focus today: <most-urgent item>'). dailyPlan stays exactly as-is as an optional pinned layer below. TodayWidget itself is untouched.

Why distinct: this variant keeps Today (the widget) intact and adds the brief as a SEPARATE home-level banner, and reuses the welcome overlay as the only intake surface. Smallest blast radius, fastest to ship, TypeScript-green by construction. The bold-ness is in the seeded, narrated landing, not in restructuring widgets.

variants.name: Conversational Quickstart -> Topics-as-data, Brief replaces the empty Today
variants.approach:
BOLDER restructure of BOTH halves. Replace the chip grid with a short conversational quickstart, and make the Brief literally BE the Today widget's empty-state so there is never a 'Nothing planned today' shell.

(1) Cold start: a new full-screen Quickstart that runs right after theme pick. Three quick conversational prompts rendered as large tap targets: 'What are you mainly juggling right now?' (multi-select life-areas), 'When's your next big thing due?' (a couple of free-text rows parsed by the EXISTING extractDeadline in taskParser.ts so 'essay friday' becomes a real dated item), and 'Anything to remember?' (free text -> addToInbox). Each selected area becomes a real Topic via worldToTopic (bundled world when one matches, synthesized otherwise) carrying description+keywords for predictTopic; the dated rows become addTopicItem calls routed by predictTopic against the just-created topics (so the user watches their own words land in the right colour-coded topic - the wow moment). Folders: if the user picks 3+ areas, group study-ish ones under a worldToFolder('study') folder so the sidebar reads structured, not flat. Finish by applying the 'student' STARTER_TEMPLATE and dismissing onboarding.

(2) Morning Brief: instead of a separate banner, REWRITE TodayWidget so its top section is an auto-synthesised brief block (next class from todayEvents, most-urgent deadline + live countdown, weather, one focus line) that ALWAYS renders from todayEvents/topic deadlines, and the TasksSection's 'Nothing planned today' branch (TodayWidget.tsx:161) is replaced by an auto-suggested plan: if dailyPlan[todayKey] is empty, surface the top N most-urgent topic items (derived from topics) as 'suggested for today' with a one-tap 'pin to plan' that writes into dailyPlan. dailyPlan becomes the optional pinned layer ON TOP of an always-useful auto view. The Today widget is now never empty for anyone, new or existing.

Why distinct: the brief lives INSIDE Today (not a separate banner), the intake is conversational and parses real deadlines via existing parser rather than canned samples, and it introduces folders + auto-suggested plan. Higher ambition, touches TodayWidget internals, still pure frontend reuse.

variants.name: Pick-a-Life-Template gallery + standalone narrated Brief hero
variants.approach:
MOST AMBITIOUS / most opinionated. Lean entirely on the existing Worlds template system for cold start, and make the Brief a full narrated hero that owns the top of home.

(1) Cold start: after theme pick, drop the user into a 'Pick how you want to start' gallery built from BUNDLED_WORLDS templates (Study, Job Hunt free; others teased). Selecting one (or several) does the full worldToTopic seed INCLUDING the world's bespoke widgetLayout, stages (e.g. the Job Hunt pipeline: To apply/Applied/Interviewing/Offer/Rejected), accent, icon, and pageBg - so the user gets fully-built topic PAGES, not just a topic shell. Pre-fill each seeded topic with 2-3 realistic dated sample items (via addTopicItem) so deadlines, mini-calendars and the brief all have content immediately, and write description+keywords for predictTopic. Multiple picks auto-nest under worldToFolder folders. A 'start blank' escape hatch keeps the current empty path. Onboarding's 4 walkthroughs are demoted to an optional 'show me around' link rather than the default gate.

(2) Morning Brief: a dedicated, visually-loud MorningBrief hero at the very top of home (replacing the plain greeting() text line), full-width, that NARRATES the day in sentences rather than lists: 'It's Tuesday. Your next thing is Lecture at 14:00, in 2h 10m. Your most urgent deadline is Essay, due Friday. It's 12C and clearing. If you do one thing today, make it the essay.' Live countdown via setInterval, weather via the existing fetch, all derived from todayEvents + topic deadline aggregation. Below the hero, the normal grid (with TodayWidget) renders unchanged; dailyPlan remains the optional pinned layer surfaced inside TodayWidget as today. The hero makes 'your morning in 90 seconds' literally true the instant the populated dashboard loads.

Why distinct: cold start is template-gallery-first (full pages + pipelines + folders, maximum day-one richness) rather than chip/conversational; the Brief is a prose-narrated hero that owns the page header rather than a compact banner or an in-widget block. Biggest visual swing, most reuse of the Worlds catalog, deliberately bold per the v2 aggressive mandate.

experiments.winner: Conversational Quickstart -> Topics-as-data, Brief replaces the empty Today

votes.preferred: Conversational Quickstart -> Topics-as-data, Brief replaces the empty Today
votes.why:
As Maya, a first-year, minute one is won the instant I see MY OWN words become a real, dated, colour-coded thing, not when the screen fills with furniture. This variant is the only one where I type "essay friday" and the existing extractDeadline (taskParser.ts, real and robust) turns it into a genuine dated item that lands in a Study topic I just created via worldToTopic, routed by predictTopic. topicScore (taskParser.ts:207-234) confirms the seeded description/keywords make that routing fire (keywords +6, description-terms +3), so I literally watch my deadline drop into the right topic. That is a personal wow; the other two seed canned sample deadlines ("essay Friday" I never typed), which reads as a demo and makes me trust it less.

Crucially, it ships minute-one and auto-Today as the SAME moment: the brief lives INSIDE TodayWidget and its empty branch ("Nothing planned today.", TodayWidget.tsx:161) is replaced by an auto-suggested plan of my most-urgent items with one-tap pin to dailyPlan. So Today is never an empty shell for me OR for existing users, and what it shows back is the deadline I just spoke. The conversational prompts ("What are you juggling", "next big thing due", "anything to remember" -> addToInbox) ask the exact things on a stressed first-year's mind, as large tap targets, with no upfront commitment.

I rejected the chip variant (TIGHTEST, lowest blast radius, and the separate brief banner is nice) because its cold start fills the home with Bozz's words, not mine, so it wins nothing emotionally in minute one. I rejected the template gallery + prose hero (best screenshot) because forcing a first-year to "pick how you want to start" from a gallery is a commitment decision before I understand the app, it dumps fully-built pages of sample data I must clean out, and the prose hero narrates fake events on day one. This variant's real cost is touching TodayWidget internals and depending on the parser, but the parser already exists, todayEvents is already aggregated and passed in ctx (Dashboard.tsx:750), and the empty-state branch is a single clean target, so the risk is acceptable and the payoff (my deadline, landed and reflected back) is the one that actually makes "your morning in 90 seconds" feel true to me.

votes.preferred: Conversational Quickstart -> Topics-as-data, Brief replaces the empty Today
votes.why: As Tom (freelancer), this is the only variant where both halves of the goal reinforce each other instead of fighting. (1) The conversational intake asks "what are you juggling / when's your next big thing due / anything to remember," and the "due" rows are parsed by the EXISTING extractDeadline (taskParser.ts) then routed by predictTopic, so I watch my OWN words ("invoice Acme friday") land dated and colour-coded in the right topic. That is the real minute-one wow, and it's real data, not the canned sample deadlines the chip variant seeds (a freelancer's first impression should be MY invoices/client calls, not a fake "Lecture" I have to delete). (2) It fixes the thing that actually kills daily return for freelancers: it rewrites TodayWidget so the "Nothing planned today" empty branch (TodayWidget.tsx:161) becomes an auto-suggested plan from my most-urgent topic items with one-tap pin-to-plan, plus a top brief block from the already-aggregated todayEvents (Dashboard.tsx:750). So Today auto-generates for new AND returning users, never an empty shell. That is literally "your morning in 90 seconds." I rejected the template-gallery+hero variant precisely because, for a freelancer, its cold start leans on BUNDLED_WORLDS where only Study and Job Hunt are free and everything freelancer-shaped (Deep Work, Shopping) is Plus-locked/teased, so minute one feels like a paywall; worse, its prose hero sits ABOVE an UNCHANGED TodayWidget, so the contradicting "Nothing planned today" shell still renders right under a hero that says "if you do one thing, make it the essay." The chip+banner variant is the safest but bolts a banner over the same untouched empty Today and uses canned samples. Caveat that applies equally to all three and is not a differentiator: worldToTopic (worlds.ts:296) hardcodes keywords:[] and ignores description, so every variant must patch description+keywords onto each seeded Topic for predictTopic to score >1; Topic.description/keywords do exist (types.ts:67-69) and topicScore weights them (+3/+6), so this is straightforward. The TodayWidget-internals edit is the only added risk, but it's contained, pure-frontend, reuses dailyPlan/predictTopic/extractDeadline, stays TypeScript-green, and is exactly where the v2 aggressive mandate's risk budget should go.

votes.preferred: Pick-a-Life-Template gallery + standalone narrated Brief hero
votes.why:
As Sam (power user) under the v2 AGGRESSIVE mandate, this variant is the one I'd actually keep, and it's the most honest about what the codebase already supports. The cold-start half leans on the Worlds system that is already fully load-bearing: worldToTopic (worlds.ts:296-313) already returns a Topic carrying stages, widgetLayout, pageBg, icon and accent, and worldToFolder (329) already exists. So "fully-built topic PAGES, not just a shell" plus the Job Hunt pipeline (the real 5-stage To apply/Applied/Interviewing/Offer/Rejected board, worlds.ts:145-151) plus auto-nesting under folders is reuse, not new infrastructure. That is the genuine wow for a power user: I pick "Job Hunt" and land on a built pipeline page with recruiter-email and deadline widgets, not a chip that quietly becomes an empty topic. The chip grid (variant 1) and the conversational quickstart (variant 2) both throw away that bespoke widgetLayout/stages richness and seed flatter topics, which for a power user is the timid read the mandate tells us to avoid.

The Brief half being a prose-narrated hero that OWNS the page header ("It's Tuesday. Your next thing is Lecture at 14:00, in 2h 10m...") is what makes "your morning in 90 seconds" literally true the instant home loads, and it reads from the already-aggregated todayEvents (Dashboard.tsx:750, topicDeadlineEvents+feed+notes). Crucially it replaces the plain greeting() line rather than stacking yet another band above the grid, so it does not push the grid down or compete with TodayWidget. Variant 1's banner is the safe-but-additive "third thing on the page" that a power user scrolls past; variant 3's hero is the page.

Two grounded reasons I rejected the others rather than just rating ambition. Variant 2 claims it reuses "the EXISTING extractDeadline" to parse free-text rows, but extractDeadline is NOT exported (taskParser.ts:300 is module-private; only parseVoiceTasks/predictTopic/splitIntoTasks/cleanTaskText are exported). So variant 2's headline mechanic either needs a new export or has to route single rows through parseVoiceTasks, which is tuned for voice rambles (heavy filler-stripping, conjunction-splitting) and will mangle a terse "essay friday" row, undercutting the very "watch your words land" moment it sells, AND it touches TodayWidget internals (rewriting the line-161 empty-state into an auto-suggested plan) which is the riskiest blast radius of the three. Variant 3 keeps TodayWidget completely untouched and adds the hero as a separate header element, so it is bold in VISUAL swing while staying low-risk in code surface, which is exactly the trade a power user wants. The one caveat I'd flag (true for variants 1 and 3 alike) is that greeting() renders at two sites (HomeView.tsx:223 mobile and 314 desktop), so the hero is two insertions not one, but that is trivial and does not change the pick. Net: variant 3 delivers maximum day-one richness from real templates and a header that earns the north-star promise, with TodayWidget left intact, so it is both the boldest and the most defensibly shippable.

experiments.priority: Ship the native recurring timetable, end to end

variants.name: Variant A — Recurrence on CalendarNote, expanded through the real ical.js engine (the literal spec)
variants.approach: The faithful, lowest-surprise build. (1) DATA: add an optional `recur?: { freq: 'weekly' | 'biweekly'; days: number[] /*0-6 Mon-first*/; termStart: number; termEnd: number }` field to CalendarNote (types.ts:279-290). Optional means every existing note stays valid and the persisted-key whitelist is untouched. (2) EXPANSION via the engine that already exists: refactor ical.ts so the bounded RRULE iterator (lines 32-53: ical.js Event.iterator + bounded window + MAX_OCCURRENCES guard) is callable as a small helper, then add `recurringNoteEvents(notes)` in calendar.ts that, for each note with `recur`, synthesizes a minimal VEVENT string (DTSTART = termStart+startMin, RRULE FREQ=WEEKLY;INTERVAL=1|2;BYDAY=MO,WE,...;UNTIL=termEnd) and feeds it through that same `parseICal`. This is the strongest read of 'reusing the bounded-window RRULE expander that already lives in ical.ts (line 6)': one expander, one bounded window, identical occurrence semantics for both subscribed feeds and native classes, so there is exactly one place recurrence can ever be wrong. The result merges in wherever `noteEvents` is already consumed (CalendarView events memo line 720, Dashboard todayEvents line 750), so the class instantly appears in month/week/day, TodayScheduleWidget, and the day panel with no per-surface work. (3) FLOW: extend the existing CreateEventForm (CalendarView.tsx:52) with a 'Repeats' section — a weekly/biweekly toggle, seven Mon-first day chips, and two date inputs for term start/end — so 'Add class' is the same form students already use, just with recurrence revealed. (4) WIDGET: new `timetable` TimetableWidget — a Mon-Fri column grid (reuse the TimeGrid layout math from CalendarView, GRID_START_HOUR/HOUR_PX) rendering only this week's expanded class instances, registered in WIDGET_REGISTRY/WidgetType. (5) FIX THE LIE: swap worlds.ts:131 from `miniCalendar` to `timetable`, rewrite the :123 description to actually promise a timetable, and sweep its em-dash to a comma. Best when correctness and a single source of truth matter most; the cost is the VEVENT-string round-trip, which is slightly indirect but keeps feed and class recurrence provably identical.

variants.name: Variant B — A first-class ClassBlock model with a thin, self-contained weekly expander (timetable as its own object)
variants.approach: Treat a recurring class as a distinct domain object rather than a flavor of calendar note, because a class is conceptually different (it has a subject/room, recurs for a term, and is never a one-off). (1) DATA: add a new `ClassBlock` type — `{ id, title, room?, color, day: number /*0-6*/, startMin, endMin, freq: 'weekly'|'biweekly', termStart, termEnd }` — and a `classBlocks: ClassBlock[]` array on the same persistence path as calendarNotes (add one string to the Dashboard.tsx:166 whitelist, mirror the existing load/save effects at :248 and :395). (2) EXPANSION: because a fixed-weekday term schedule is far simpler than general RRULE, write a tight ~30-line `expandClassBlocks(blocks, rangeStart, rangeEnd)` in a new lib/timetable.ts that walks weeks from termStart to termEnd, emits a CalendarEvent per matching weekday (skipping odd weeks for biweekly), and reuses the exact same bounded window constants exported from ical.ts (EXPAND_BACK_DAYS/EXPAND_FWD_DAYS/MAX_OCCURRENCES) so the window stays consistent without forcing class data through ical.js string parsing. The output is CalendarEvent[] with `source: 'note'`, so again it slots into every existing consumer for free. (3) FLOW: a dedicated, student-shaped 'Add class' modal — subject name, color, room, day chips, time range, term dates — separate from the generic event form, so the #1 action has a purpose-built UI instead of being buried in event-creation. (4) WIDGET: TimetableWidget reads classBlocks directly (no event round-trip needed for the widget's own week view), making it cheap to render the recurring skeleton even with zero one-off events. (5) FIX: same worlds.ts:131/:123 relabel + em-dash sweep, and the Study template gains a real timetable surface. Best when you want the timetable to become a durable product primitive (the natural anchor the deferred Grades/GPA tracker can later hang module names off of); the tradeoff is a second small expander to keep in step with the feed one, and a touch more wiring than A.

variants.name: Variant C — Timetable-first onboarding: a paste/import 'set up your week' builder over the Variant-A model
variants.approach: Same minimal data model as Variant A (optional `recur` on CalendarNote, expanded through the existing ical.ts engine — no new dependency, no schema break) but the bold bet is on COLD START, the exact failure the board flagged for the deferred GPA tracker: a timetable widget is worthless empty, and entering 6-10 classes one modal at a time is the real drop-off. So the headline feature is a fast bulk builder. (1) A `timetable` TimetableWidget whose empty state is not 'nothing here' but an inline 'Build your week' affordance. (2) A TimetableSetup flow with two on-ramps: a structured grid editor (add row: subject, day chips, start/end, room — repeat) that commits all rows as recurring CalendarNotes in one action with a single shared term start/end picked once; and a paste box that parses common copied-timetable text (e.g. lines like 'Mon 09:00 Calculus B12', tab/comma/space separated) into prefilled rows the student confirms before saving — turning a screenshot-or-portal-copy into a populated week in seconds. (3) The same TimetableWidget weekly grid as A for display, plus this is the surface the Study world points at: worlds.ts:131 -> `timetable`, :123 rewritten to promise a real timetable, em-dash swept. (4) Because it reuses A's note+recur+ical.ts expansion underneath, every class still appears across calendar, today, and day panel with no extra plumbing. Best when activation/retention is the priority over model purity: it directly answers 'an empty timetable is wasted' and seeds enough data that a later Grades tracker lands on a populated term. The tradeoff is the largest UI build of the three (a parser + grid editor) and looser paste parsing that must stay confirm-before-save to avoid wrong rows.

experiments.winner: Variant C — Timetable-first onboarding: a paste/import 'set up your week' builder over the Variant-A model

votes.preferred: Variant C — Timetable-first onboarding: a paste/import 'set up your week' builder over the Variant-A model
votes.why:
I'm Maya, a first-year. Here's the honest truth about how I'd actually use this. The reason I'd add a timetable at all is to stop re-checking my uni portal every morning — that's "my morning in 90 seconds" working. But a timetable is only useful once it has my 6-10 classes in it. An empty grid does nothing for me, and that's exactly where I'd bounce.

That's why A and B lose me even though they're cleaner engineering. Both make me add classes one at a time through a form/modal. I have like five subjects with two-or-three weekly slots each — that's a dozen-plus trips through "open form, pick day chips, type time, pick term dates, save." I will do that maybe twice and then quit, and now I've got a half-empty timetable that's worse than useless because I can't trust it. The single-source-of-truth elegance of A and the durable ClassBlock primitive of B are real, but they're solving the developer's problem (where recurrence lives), not mine (getting my actual week in without it being a chore).

Variant C is the only one that respects how I'd really populate it. I already have my timetable somewhere — a screenshot from the portal, a PDF, an email, a copied block of text. The paste box that turns "Mon 09:00 Calculus B12" lines into prefilled rows I confirm is exactly the on-ramp I'd use: my whole week populated in seconds instead of fifteen modal sessions. And the structured grid editor with one shared term-start/end picked ONCE is huge — in A and B I'd be re-entering the same term dates on every single class, which is maddening. The confirm-before-save step on the paste parser is the right call; loose parsing scares me, but reviewing prefilled rows before they commit means a wrong guess costs me a tap, not a corrupted week.

The risk people will raise is that C is the biggest UI build (parser + grid editor) and the parsing is fuzzy. I buy that as a build cost, but as the user it's the cost that's on the developer's side, not mine — and crucially C is built ON Variant A's model (optional recur on CalendarNote, expanded through the existing ical.ts engine), so it keeps A's "every class shows up in calendar, today, and the day panel for free" and its no-schema-break safety. I get A's correctness foundation AND the activation layer that makes the feature non-empty on day one. B's separate ClassBlock primitive is the most "right" long-term and would be the better anchor for a future grades/GPA tracker, but I don't care about a future tracker today; I care that the timetable I set up this week is full and trustworthy, and that a later tracker lands on a populated term — which is precisely what C seeds. For a first-year deciding whether this app earns a place in my morning, the one that gets my real classes in fast wins. That's C.

votes.preferred: Variant C — Timetable-first onboarding: a paste/import 'set up your week' builder over the Variant-A model
votes.why:
As Tom the freelancer, I'm not the canonical student here, but the way I actually live in a tool decides everything: a feature only earns its place if it's populated within my first sitting, and a recurring weekly skeleton (client calls Tue/Thu, gym, standing standup) is exactly the kind of thing I'll set up once or never. That's why C wins for me, and the codebase backs up why it's the safe bold bet rather than the reckless one.

The cold-start problem is the real risk, not the data model. I verified that all three variants converge on the same end-state plumbing: noteEvents(calendarNotes) is spread in precisely two consumers (Dashboard.tsx:754 todayEvents and CalendarView.tsx:724 events memo), so any recurring source that merges into noteEvents shows up across month/week/day, TodayScheduleWidget and the day panel with zero per-surface work. The bounded RRULE expander genuinely exists at ical.ts:32-53 and is the one place recurrence semantics live. So the engineering question 'will recurrence render everywhere correctly' is essentially solved by A's substrate, which C reuses verbatim (optional recur on CalendarNote, no schema break, no new dependency, persisted-key whitelist at Dashboard.tsx:166 untouched). Given that the hard part is already de-risked, the marginal value of B's separate ClassBlock model is low for me, and the marginal value of C's bulk builder is high: it's the only variant that attacks the thing that actually kills timetable widgets, which is the empty grid. The board already flagged this exact failure for the deferred GPA tracker; shipping a timetable with the same trap baked in would just relocate the drop-off.

Why not A: A is the most defensible build and the single-source-of-truth argument is genuinely strong, but its activation story is 'open the same CreateEventForm I already know (CalendarView.tsx:52), now with a Repeats section, once per class.' For a 6-10 entry week that's 6-10 trips through a modal, and realistically I bail after two. A ships a correct timetable that stays empty, which for a 'your morning in 90 seconds' product is a quiet failure.

Why not B: a first-class ClassBlock is the right call only if the timetable must become a durable primitive that a future Grades/GPA tracker hangs off, and it's the cleanest model on paper. But it buys a second expander (lib/timetable.ts) that has to be kept in lockstep with the ical.ts one forever, plus more persistence wiring, and it still does nothing about cold start. For an aggressive dogfood round whose goal is 'ship end to end' and prove the feature gets used, paying model-purity tax while leaving the activation gap open is the wrong trade. The Grades-anchor benefit is speculative and deferred; the empty-widget problem is present and certain.

C's tradeoff is honest and acceptable: it's the largest UI build (grid editor plus a paste parser), and loose paste parsing is the danger. But the proposal already contains the right guardrail (confirm-before-save on parsed rows), and the structured grid editor with a single shared term-start/end picked once is a contained, well-scoped piece of React, fully self-contained, no auth/payments/schema/CI exposure. Crucially, C still delivers everything A delivers (same recur+ical.ts expansion, same TimetableWidget weekly grid, same worlds.ts:131 miniCalendar->timetable swap and :123 rewrite that I confirmed currently over-promises 'a timetable' while pointing at miniCalendar) and adds the one thing that turns a shipped feature into a used one. For a freelancer or a student, 'paste my schedule and have a populated week in seconds' is the moment the app earns the home screen. That's the bold, high-leverage move this round is asking for.

votes.preferred: Variant B — A first-class ClassBlock model with a thin, self-contained weekly expander (timetable as its own object)
votes.why:
As a power user shipping a timetable "end to end," I care most about the model being right, because everything downstream (the deferred Grades/GPA tracker, room/subject metadata, term-aware editing) hangs off it. A class is not a flavor of calendar note: it has a room, a subject, a term span, and a weekday rhythm, and it is never a one-off. Modeling it as ClassBlock makes the #1 student action a real domain object instead of an optional `recur` bag bolted onto CalendarNote, and I verified the integration cost B claims is genuinely small: its output is CalendarEvent[] with source:'note', so it slots into the exact same single merge point I confirmed in the code (calendar.ts:28 noteEvents -> CalendarView.tsx:724 events memo), meaning month/week/day/today all light up for free just like A. The persistence claim also checks out: it is one string added to the Dashboard whitelist (Dashboard.tsx:161-168) mirroring the calendarNotes load/save path that already exists.

My main worry with B was "a second expander to keep in step with the feed one," and that is real, but I weighed it against A's actual cost and found A worse for me. A's headline pitch is "one expander, provably identical semantics," but the way it achieves that is by synthesizing a VEVENT *string* per class and round-tripping it through ICAL.parse. For a fixed-weekday term schedule that is the wrong tool: I'd be hand-building RRULE/BYDAY/UNTIL strings, getting timezone/DTSTART and biweekly INTERVAL anchoring exactly right, and debugging through ical.js when a Tuesday slips. That round-trip is a bigger and more fragile surface than B's ~30-line week-walker that just reuses the exported window constants (EXPAND_BACK_DAYS/FWD/MAX_OCCURRENCES from ical.ts) for consistency without forcing class data through a string parser. So B's "second expander" is actually simpler and easier to keep correct than A's "shared" one, and the timetable widget reads classBlocks directly with no event round-trip for its own week view.

I rejected C even though its cold-start insight is correct (an empty timetable is dead weight): it is the largest UI build of the three (paste parser + grid editor) and it doubles down on A's note+recur+VEVENT model, so it inherits A's fragile expansion AND adds loose paste parsing on top. For an "end to end, ship it" round, that is too much surface and too much risk in the activation layer for round one. C's bulk-import builder is the right *follow-up* once the durable primitive exists, and B is the only variant that gives that builder a clean object (classBlocks) to write into later. All three correctly fix the worlds.ts:131 miniCalendar->timetable lie and the em-dash; B is the one that also turns the Study template into a durable product surface rather than a relabeled calendar.

changes.title: Add an optional time-of-day to deadlines (dueMin field), end to end
changes.approach: Additive, migration-safe. Add `dueMin: number | null` (minutes from local midnight, null = all-day) to TopicItem (types.ts:21-32) and ListItem (types.ts:491-499). The stored `deadline` stays unix-ms-at-local-midnight as the date anchor, so every existing record (dueMin undefined→treated as null) keeps working with zero migration. Wire DeadlineControl to pass `showTime` into the existing DatePicker (DatePicker.tsx already fully supports a time picker at lines 201-228 and encodes hour/min into the timestamp via buildTimestamp) — on pick, split the chosen timestamp into the midnight `deadline` plus a derived `dueMin`, and seed the picker's initial time back from `deadline+dueMin` so reopening shows the set time. Update topicDeadlineEvents (calendar.ts:5-25) to emit a TIMED CalendarEvent when dueMin!=null (allDay:false, start=deadline+dueMin*60000, startMin=dueMin) and keep allDay:true otherwise, so timed deadlines flow into todayEvents/calendar correctly. Extend DeadlineEntry in util.ts to carry dueMin so downstream widgets can render exact times. This is the foundation every other deadline change depends on; ship first.
changes.risk: Low — purely additive optional field; the midnight-ms path is untouched when dueMin is null, so old data and the existing all-day rendering are unaffected. Main care point is consistently reconstructing the full timestamp (deadline+dueMin) wherever the picker reads/writes.

changes.title: Rebuild UpcomingDeadlines into a live red/amber/green urgency engine
changes.approach: This is the user-chosen winner: 'live-urgency engine first'. Add an urgency helper to dates.ts: given a deadline (and optional dueMin) it returns {bucket:'overdue'|'red'|'amber'|'green', label} where label is a live countdown — 'overdue 2d', 'due in 4h', 'in 3d' — computed from the full timestamp (deadline + dueMin*60000), falling back to end-of-day when dueMin is null so all-day items still bucket sanely. Rewrite UpcomingDeadlinesWidget (currently day-bucketed, UpcomingDeadlinesWidget.tsx:35-104) to sort by exact timestamp and render each item as an urgency row: a colour-coded left bar/pill (overdue+red use t.alert, amber uses t.doingAccent/amber, green uses t.doneAccent), the live countdown label, and the exact time when dueMin is set ('Fri 17:00'). Add a `useState`+`setInterval` ticking every 60s so 'due in 4h' counts down without a reload (clear on unmount). Keep the existing accent-dot + setActiveSection navigation. This is what turns Bozz from a to-do list into a trustworthy deadline tracker.
changes.risk: Low-Medium — self-contained widget rewrite plus one pure helper. The only runtime concern is the 60s interval; guard it with cleanup. Visual-only, no data writes.

changes.title: Teach extractDeadline to read clock times ('5pm', '23:59', 'by noon')
changes.approach: Extend the offline parser so smart capture can set a timed deadline. Add a `timeMin?: number` to the DeadlineResult type (taskParser.ts:279) and a time-phrase matcher inside extractDeadline (taskParser.ts:300-396) that recognises '5pm', '5:30pm', '17:00', '23:59', 'noon', 'midnight', 'at 9' and converts to minutes-from-midnight. Combine it with whatever day the existing date matchers resolve (today/tomorrow/weekday/etc): when a time is found, return timeMin so callers populate dueMin. Add the matched time substring to the strip regex so it's removed from the task text. Surface timeMin through parseVoiceTasks → ParsedTask (add dueMin) and through InboxItem so triage carries the time into the topic item.
changes.risk: Medium — regex parsing on free text; scope the time matcher tightly (require am/pm, a colon, or an explicit 'at'/'by' anchor) to avoid swallowing numbers like 'buy 5 apples'. Additive to the result type, so no existing call path breaks if no time is found.

changes.title: Cold-start: Conversational Quickstart that seeds real topics + sample timed deadlines
changes.approach: User-chosen winner: 'Conversational Quickstart -> topics-as-data, Brief replaces empty Today'. Add a new Quickstart step that runs right after the theme pick (Dashboard wires showWelcome at 1382 and chooseWelcomeMood at 340-344). New self-contained component (Quickstart.tsx, modelled on WelcomeThemePicker's full-screen portal) asks 1-2 quick chip questions ('What are you here for?' → Uni / Work / Job hunt / Personal, multi-select). On finish it seeds real topics by reusing worldToTopic/worldToFolder (worlds.ts:296-338) from the matching BUNDLED_WORLDS (study, job-hunt) — each topic gets a `description` so predictTopic works day one (taskParser.ts:226-234 reads description terms) — plus 2-3 sample TopicItems carrying timed deadlines (deadline+dueMin from change #1, e.g. an essay 'due Fri 17:00'). Push via setTopics/setTopicFolders (Dashboard.tsx:102-103) and replace DEFAULT_HOME with a populated starter (the Student STARTER_TEMPLATE already exists, registry.tsx:125-136). Mark welcomeComplete so it never re-shows. The user lands on a populated, personal dashboard instead of empty shells.
changes.risk: Medium — touches the first-run path. Keep it strictly additive: only fires for brandNewAccount (the existing guard at Dashboard.tsx:306-308), is skippable, and seeds via the already-tested world helpers. No auth/payment/schema involvement.

changes.title: Make Today an auto-synthesised Morning Brief (no manual dailyPlan required)
changes.approach: Second half of the chosen cold-start move: 'Brief replaces the empty Today'. Today currently shows 'Nothing planned today' until the user manually fills dailyPlan (TodayWidget.tsx:144,161-170). Add an auto-brief header section above the existing Events/Tasks that synthesises from data already in WidgetCtx (context.ts): the next timed event today (todayEvents, already passed), the single most-urgent deadline with the live countdown from change #2 (via deadlineEntries, util.ts), the day/date, and one narrated focus line ('Most urgent: Essay — due in 4h'). Keep dailyPlan as an optional pinned layer below (unchanged TasksSection), and change the empty Tasks copy so it never reads as a dead end. Gate the brief behind a new config key (showBrief, default true) consistent with the existing showEvents/showTasks pattern (TodayWidget.tsx:235-236).
changes.risk: Low — reuses ctx data the widget already receives; no new wiring into Dashboard. Pure presentation; degrades gracefully when there are no events/deadlines.

changes.title: Ship a native recurring timetable (recur on CalendarNote) with a 'set up your week' builder, and fix the Study mislabel
changes.approach: User-chosen Variant C over the Variant-A model: recurrence on CalendarNote, expanded at render, with a paste/builder onboarding. (1) Add an optional `recur?: { freq:'weekly'|'biweekly'; days:number[]; termStart:number; termEnd:number }` to CalendarNote (types.ts:279-290) — undefined = today's one-off behaviour, fully migration-safe. (2) In calendar.ts add expandRecurringNotes(notes, rangeStart, rangeEnd) that walks each recurring note across the bounded window (mirror ical.ts's bounded approach at ical.ts:6-12: cap occurrences, clamp to termStart/termEnd) and emits one CalendarEvent per class occurrence, keeping startMin/endMin; fold it into noteEvents so timetable classes appear in todayEvents and the calendar with no new render layer. (3) New TimetableWidget.tsx: a real Mon-Sun weekly grid reading the recurring notes, plus a simple 'Add class' form (title, day(s), start/end time, term range) and a paste box that parses pasted lines like 'Mon 9-11 Calculus' into recurring notes — wire it via the calendarNotes/onCalendarNotesChange already in WidgetCtx (context.ts:33-35). Register the widget in registry.tsx (add 'timetable' to WidgetType in types.ts:320-326 and to WIDGET_REGISTRY). (4) Fix worlds.ts: relabel the Study world's miniCalendar comment/usage so 'timetable' means the real TimetableWidget (worlds.ts:131), and sweep the em/en dashes in worlds.ts descriptions (e.g. worlds.ts:123) to commas/colons per house style.
changes.risk: Medium — the new widget and the bounded expander are the bulk of the work; the data change is an optional field. Keep the expander bounded (occurrence cap + term clamp) to avoid runaway loops, and ensure non-recurring notes (recur undefined) take the existing single-day path unchanged.

plan.summary:
Six bold-but-safe, fully frontend changes implementing the three agenda priorities with the user-chosen variants, all grounded in real files.

Foundation (deadlines with time, made alive): #1 adds an optional `dueMin: number | null` to TopicItem/ListItem (types.ts:28-29,498), keeping the midnight-ms anchor so migration is a no-op; it reuses DatePicker's already-built time picker (DatePicker.tsx:201-228) and teaches topicDeadlineEvents (calendar.ts) to emit timed events. #2 (the chosen 'live-urgency engine first' variant) rewrites UpcomingDeadlinesWidget into a ticking red/amber/green countdown via a new dates.ts helper. #3 teaches extractDeadline (taskParser.ts:300-396) to read '5pm'/'23:59' so smart capture sets a time.

Win minute one + auto-Today (chosen 'Conversational Quickstart -> Brief replaces empty Today'): #4 adds a post-theme Quickstart that seeds real described topics + sample timed deadlines by reusing worldToTopic/worldToFolder (worlds.ts:296-338), replacing the empty DEFAULT_HOME (registry.tsx:111). #5 turns TodayWidget into an auto-synthesised Morning Brief from data already in WidgetCtx, so it's never empty (fixes TodayWidget.tsx:161-170).

Recurring timetable (chosen Variant C): #6 adds an optional `recur` to CalendarNote (types.ts:279-290), a bounded expander in calendar.ts modelled on ical.ts:6-12, a real weekly TimetableWidget with an 'Add class'/paste builder, registers it, and fixes the Study world's 'timetable' mislabel + em-dash copy (worlds.ts:123,131).

Every change is additive and TypeScript-green by construction (new optional fields, no removed shapes), and respects all hard constraints — no auth, payments, secrets, schema, or CI touched. Deferred per the board: Grades/GPA tracker (take it after these land).
